title: Product Detail Page to Cart Rate
when_to_use: assess product page effectiveness and content quality
benchmark product categories or individual items
diagnose friction in product page-to-action journey
evaluate impact of A/B tests on product page variants
identify underperforming SKUs for optimization
business_definition: Percentage of product detail page views that resulted in an add-to-cart action in the same session. A page view is defined as a view of a product detail page (PDVP), and an add-to-cart is an event where the customer added at least one item to their cart.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: How effectively product pages convert visitors into cart adders — a mid-funnel indicator of product appeal, pricing clarity, and page experience.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Purchase intent or conversion. A customer may add to cart and abandon, or abandon immediately after — this only measures initial cart interest.
metric_nature.common_misconception: PDP to cart rate is not the same as cart abandonment rate. PDP to cart measures *entry* to the cart step; abandonment measures *drop-off* after entry.

template: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.customer_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) AS pdp_to_cart_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id) AS total_pdp_views, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.customer_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN cart_adds c ON p.session_id = c.session_id AND p.product_id = c.product_id WHERE p.view_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}

inflation_causes.cause: Counting add-to-cart events per item instead of per session
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the numerator counts *items* added to cart instead of *sessions* with at least one add-to-cart, sessions with multiple items (e.g., bulk order) are counted multiple times — inflating the rate above 100%.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT session_id, COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) AS items_added, COUNT(*) AS add_to_cart_events FROM cart_adds GROUP BY session_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 ORDER BY add_to_cart_events DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Count distinct sessions with at least one add-to-cart, not total add events: `COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN add_to_cart = 1 THEN session_id END)`.

inflation_causes.cause: Including PDP views from bots or crawlers
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If PDP views table includes non-human traffic (bots, SEO crawlers), and cart adds table filters only humans, the denominator inflates disproportionately — deflating rate. However, if both include bots but bots never add carts, the *relative* effect is deflation, but if bots sometimes add carts via API, the numerator may inflate slightly — net effect: erratic volatility.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS total_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN is_bot = true THEN session_id END) AS bot_sessions FROM pdp_views WHERE view_date >= '2024-01-01'
inflation_causes.fix: Filter both PDP views and cart adds to exclude known bots using a user-agent or bot_score filter.

inflation_causes.cause: Cart event timestamp mismatch with PDP view session
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If cart adds are recorded *after* the session ends (e.g., delayed ETL), they are matched to incorrect sessions or future sessions — inflating or deflating PDP-to-cart for the current period depending on data latency.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT session_id, view_time, add_time, DATEDIFF(minute, view_time, add_time) AS minutes_diff FROM pdp_views p JOIN cart_adds c ON p.session_id = c.session_id WHERE add_time > view_time AND add_time > DATEADD(hour, 1, view_time) ORDER BY minutes_diff DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Match cart adds to PDP views using the *session_id* and enforce a narrow time window (e.g., add within 15 minutes of view in the same session).

deflation_causes.cause: Excluding mobile sessions with cart sync delay
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If mobile cart syncing is delayed (e.g., cart stored locally and synced after page exit), cart events are recorded in a new session — PDP view in session A, add-to-cart in session B, and the conversion is missed.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN device_type = 'mobile' AND cart_sync_delay_minutes > 5 THEN session_id END) AS delayed_mobile_adds FROM cart_adds WHERE add_date >= '2024-01-01'
deflation_causes.fix: Attribute cart adds to the original session using persistent user_id or device_id cross-session, or extend the session window (e.g., 30-minute inactivity timeout).

diagnostic_questions: Is the rate anomaly concentrated in specific products, categories, or SKUs?
Has a new product image, description, or pricing display been updated?
Did mobile vs desktop performance shift recently?
Are cart add events properly attributed to the original session or fragmented?
Has bot filtering logic changed (e.g., new bot signatures introduced)?

causal_relationships.if: PDP to cart rate up, conversion_rate up
causal_relationships.then: product page optimizations are driving more *and* deeper engagement — likely improved imagery, reviews, or pricing transparency

causal_relationships.if: PDP to cart rate up, AOV down
causal_relationships.then: more items added per order but lower value — likely upsell mechanics failing or promotional bundling shifting mix

causal_relationships.if: PDP to cart rate down, revenue per visitor up
causal_relationships.then: fewer cart adds but higher value per convert — likely more qualified traffic or better targeting, not page issues

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: PDP to cart rate drops >15% MoM with stable traffic volume
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Cart add events are being recorded, but checkout is not — likely a checkout flow regression (e.g., new form fields, payment failures). This shows that the drop is not in product appeal, but in friction *after* cart addition.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', p.view_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id) AS pdp_views, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) AS cart_add_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN checkout_started = true THEN p.session_id END) AS checkout_starts FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN cart_adds c ON p.session_id = c.session_id LEFT JOIN checkout_events ce ON p.session_id = ce.session_id WHERE p.view_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: PDP to cart rate spikes during a flash sale
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A time-sensitive promotion drives urgency to add items to cart, but users still abandon before checkout — likely due to shipping cost surprises, size uncertainty, or friction at the cart page itself.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', p.view_date) AS week, p.promotion_code, COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id) AS pdp_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) AS cart_adds, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) AS purchases FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN cart_adds c ON p.session_id = c.session_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.session_id = o.session_id WHERE p.view_date >= '2024-01-01' AND p.promotion_code IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1, p.promotion_code ORDER BY week, pdp_sessions DESC

anti_patterns.pattern: Using total cart adds instead of sessions with at least one add
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If a customer adds 5 items to cart in one session, this counts as 5 conversions — inflating rate above 100% and misleading the signal about page efficiency.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(c.cart_id) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN cart_adds c ON p.session_id = c.session_id
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT session_id FROM cart_adds) c ON p.session_id = c.session_id

anti_patterns.pattern: Matching cart add to *any* product PDP view in session, not specific product view
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If a session views product A, leaves, returns, and adds product B to cart, and attribution uses `session_id` only, the conversion is misattributed to product A — inflating A's conversion rate and distorting optimization priorities.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN cart_adds c ON p.session_id = c.session_id
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL AND c.product_id = p.product_id THEN p.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN (SELECT session_id, product_id FROM cart_adds) c ON p.session_id = c.session_id AND p.product_id = c.product_id

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT session_id, product_id FROM cart_adds) c ON p.session_id = c.session_id AND p.product_id = c.product_id WHERE p.view_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', p.view_date) AS month, p.category, COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id) AS pdp_views, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) AS cart_add_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM pdp_views p LEFT JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT session_id, product_id FROM cart_adds) c ON p.session_id = c.session_id AND p.product_id = c.product_id GROUP BY 1, p.category ORDER BY 1, 4 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH pdp_sessions AS (
  -- Grain: sessions with at least one PDP view
  SELECT
    session_id,
    product_id,
    category,
    MIN(view_date) AS first_view
  FROM pdp_views
  WHERE view_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY session_id, product_id, category
),
cart_sessions AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT session_id, product_id
  FROM cart_adds
),
pdp_to_cart AS (
  SELECT
    p.category,
    COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id) AS pdp_views,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN p.session_id END) AS cart_adds
  FROM pdp_sessions p
  LEFT JOIN cart_sessions c ON p.session_id = c.session_id AND p.product_id = c.product_id
  GROUP BY p.category
)
SELECT
  category,
  pdp_views,
  cart_adds,
  ROUND(cart_adds * 100.0 / NULLIF(pdp_views, 0), 2) AS pdp_to_cart_rate,
  ROUND(pdp_views / NULLIF(cart_adds, 0), 2) AS views_per_add
FROM pdp_to_cart

title: Checkout Step Drop-off Rate
when_to_use: diagnose friction points in checkout journey
prioritize checkout optimization (e.g., form reduction, payment alternatives)
benchmark checkout efficiency across device types or cohorts
measure impact of checkout experiments and A/B tests
estimate lost revenue from abandonment
business_definition: Percentage of sessions that exit the checkout funnel at each step, relative to sessions that entered that step. A checkout step is defined as: Cart, Shipping, Billing, Review, Payment, Confirmation. Drop-off is measured as (sessions entering step *n* minus sessions entering step *n+1*) ÷ sessions entering step *n*.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: Where and how severely customers abandon the checkout process — a high-friction diagnostic signal for UX and business logic issues.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Root cause of drop-off. It quantifies *where* abandonment occurs, but not *why* (e.g., shipping cost, unexpected fees, account creation).
metric_nature.common_misconception: Checkout drop-off rate is not the same as cart abandonment rate. Drop-off is step-by-step; cart abandonment is overall exit after cart entry — this metric isolates friction at each stage.

template:
WITH step_funnel AS (
  SELECT
    session_id,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_cart,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'shipping' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_shipping,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'billing' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_billing,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'review' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_review,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'payment' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_payment,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'confirmation' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_confirmation
  FROM checkout_events WHERE session_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY session_id
),
step_counts AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_cart = 1 THEN session_id END) AS cart_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_shipping = 1 THEN session_id END) AS shipping_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_billing = 1 THEN session_id END) AS billing_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_review = 1 THEN session_id END) AS review_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_payment = 1 THEN session_id END) AS payment_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_confirmation = 1 THEN session_id END) AS confirmation_entered
  FROM step_funnel
)
SELECT
  cart_entered,
  shipping_entered,
  billing_entered,
  review_entered,
  payment_entered,
  confirmation_entered,
  ROUND((cart_entered - shipping_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(cart_entered, 0), 2) AS cart_to_shipping_drop_off,
  ROUND((shipping_entered - billing_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(shipping_entered, 0), 2) AS shipping_to_billing_drop_off,
  ROUND((billing_entered - review_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(billing_entered, 0), 2) AS billing_to_review_drop_off,
  ROUND((review_entered - payment_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(review_entered, 0), 2) AS review_to_payment_drop_off,
  ROUND((payment_entered - confirmation_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(payment_entered, 0), 2) AS payment_to_confirmation_drop_off
FROM step_counts

inflation_causes.cause: Counting multi-step exits as multiple drop-offs
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a session skips step 2 and goes from step 1 to step 3, it is counted as a drop-off at step 2 *and* step 3 — inflating drop-off rates artificially. Funnel math requires step-wise progression.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT session_id, step, LAG(step) OVER (PARTITION BY session_id ORDER BY step_order) AS prev_step FROM checkout_events WHERE session_date >= '2024-01-01' ORDER BY session_id, step_order LIMIT 50
inflation_causes.fix: Use lead_lag to ensure only *sequential* step transitions are counted, or aggregate by session-level max per step (as in the template).

inflation_causes.cause: Not filtering to unique sessions per step
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a session re-enters a step (e.g., due to back navigation, form validation retry), and step counts are summed instead of `COUNT(DISTINCT)`, that session inflates the denominator for that step — deflating drop-off rate. However, if re-entries only happen *after* abandonment (e.g., retry after failure), it may slightly inflate drop-off at earlier steps — net effect: erratic volatility.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT session_id, COUNT(*) AS total_events, COUNT(DISTINCT step) AS unique_steps FROM checkout_events WHERE session_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY session_id HAVING COUNT(*) > COUNT(DISTINCT step) ORDER BY total_events DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Always use `COUNT(DISTINCT session_id)` per step to avoid double-counting re-entries.

inflation_causes.cause: Including test or internal traffic in checkout events
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If internal staff or QA sessions are included, and they abandon checkout intentionally (to test flows), drop-off rates spike in steps tested — distorting real customer abandonment signals.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT session_id, COUNT(DISTINCT step) AS steps_count, COUNT(*) AS events FROM checkout_events WHERE session_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY session_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT step) > 10 ORDER BY events DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to sessions with user_type = 'customer' or exclude known internal IPs/users before funnel aggregation.

deflation_causes.cause: Excluding mobile sessions with delayed step events
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If mobile step events are logged late (e.g., after session timeout), the session appears to have skipped steps (e.g., entered shipping directly), deflating earlier step drop-off rates.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT device_type, AVG(DATEDIFF(minute, entered_shipping, entered_billing)) AS avg_billing_delay FROM (SELECT session_id, MIN(CASE WHEN step = 'shipping' THEN event_time END) AS entered_shipping, MIN(CASE WHEN step = 'billing' THEN event_time END) AS entered_billing FROM checkout_events WHERE session_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY session_id) sub GROUP BY device_type
deflation_causes.fix: Attribute step events to session using persistent session_id and device_id, not timestamp-based session reassignment.

diagnostic_questions: Which specific checkout step shows the highest drop-off, and is it consistent across devices?
Has a new form field, policy (e.g., account creation), or payment gateway been introduced?
Is the drop-off correlated with cart value (e.g., high-value carts abandon at shipping)?
Did a shipping cost calculation error or unexpected fee appear in the period?
Are drop-offs concentrated in specific user segments (e.g., new vs returning, mobile vs desktop)?

causal_relationships.then: unexpected or increased shipping cost is driving abandonment — likely a change in carrier rates or delivery policy
new payment methods may lack trust or user familiarity — likely technical friction or incomplete implementation
high-value carts fear commitment — likely account creation friction or lack of guest checkout option

cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A carrier rate increase or new surcharge (e.g., fuel, peak season) was passed through to customers, making shipping costs visible at the shipping step and triggering abandonment.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', c.session_date) AS week, COUNT(DISTINCT c.session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'shipping' THEN c.session_id END) AS entered_shipping, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'billing' THEN c.session_id END) AS entered_billing, AVG(s.shipping_cost) AS avg_shipping FROM checkout_events c JOIN shipping_costs s ON c.session_id = s.session_id WHERE c.session_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY week

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Payment-to-Confirmation drop-off spikes during maintenance windows
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A payment gateway maintenance or rate-limiting event caused declined payments to be logged as *abandonments* instead of *failures* — misclassifying a technical error as a UX drop-off.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', e.event_time) AS day, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e.event = 'payment_declined' THEN e.session_id END) AS declines, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e.event = 'abandon_checkout' THEN e.session_id END) AS abandonments FROM events e WHERE e.event IN ('payment_declined', 'abandon_checkout') AND e.event_time >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY day

anti_patterns.why_wrong: If step counts are subtracted *per session* (e.g., 1-1=0, 1-0=1), sessions with multiple step gaps are miscounted — inflating or deflating drop-off rates unpredictably.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT session_id, SUM(CASE WHEN step = 'shipping' AND prev_step != 'cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cart_to_shipping_drop FROM (SELECT session_id, step, LAG(step) OVER (PARTITION BY session_id ORDER BY step_order) AS prev_step FROM checkout_events) sub
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_cart = 1 AND entered_shipping = 0 THEN session_id END) AS cart_to_shipping_drop FROM step_funnel

anti_patterns.pattern: Using event counts instead of session counts
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If drop-off rate is computed as (events missing step_n+1 / events at step_n), sessions with many events at step_n (e.g., retries) are overcounted — deflating drop-off rate.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE step = 'shipping') * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE step = 'cart'), 0) AS cart_to_shipping_drop FROM checkout_events
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_cart = 1 AND entered_shipping = 0 THEN session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_cart = 1 THEN session_id END), 0) AS cart_to_shipping_drop FROM step_funnel

sql_assets.basic_sql: WITH step_funnel AS (SELECT session_id, MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_cart, MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'shipping' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_shipping, MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'billing' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_billing, MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'review' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_review, MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'payment' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_payment, MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'confirmation' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_confirmation FROM checkout_events WHERE session_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY session_id), step_counts AS (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_cart = 1 THEN session_id END) AS cart_entered, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_shipping = 1 THEN session_id END) AS shipping_entered, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_billing = 1 THEN session_id END) AS billing_entered, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_review = 1 THEN session_id END) AS review_entered, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_payment = 1 THEN session_id END) AS payment_entered, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_confirmation = 1 THEN session_id END) AS confirmation_entered FROM step_funnel) SELECT cart_entered, shipping_entered, billing_entered, review_entered, payment_entered, confirmation_entered, ROUND((cart_entered - shipping_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(cart_entered, 0), 2) AS cart_to_shipping_drop_off, ROUND((shipping_entered - billing_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(shipping_entered, 0), 2) AS shipping_to_billing_drop_off FROM step_counts
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', c.session_date) AS month, c.device_type, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'cart' THEN c.session_id END) AS cart_entered, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'shipping' THEN c.session_id END) AS shipping_entered, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'billing' THEN c.session_id END) AS billing_entered, ROUND((COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'cart' THEN c.session_id END) - COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'shipping' THEN c.session_id END)) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.step = 'cart' THEN c.session_id END), 0), 2) AS cart_to_shipping_drop_off FROM checkout_events c WHERE c.session_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, c.device_type ORDER BY 1, 5 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH step_funnel AS (
  -- Grain: one row per session with step entry flags
  SELECT
    session_id,
    device_type,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_cart,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'shipping' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_shipping,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'billing' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_billing,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'review' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_review,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'payment' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_payment,
    MAX(CASE WHEN step = 'confirmation' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS entered_confirmation
  FROM checkout_events
  WHERE session_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY session_id, device_type
),
step_counts AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_cart = 1 THEN session_id END) AS cart_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_shipping = 1 THEN session_id END) AS shipping_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_billing = 1 THEN session_id END) AS billing_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_review = 1 THEN session_id END) AS review_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_payment = 1 THEN session_id END) AS payment_entered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN entered_confirmation = 1 THEN session_id END) AS confirmation_entered
  FROM step_funnel
),
funnel_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    cart_entered,
    shipping_entered,
    billing_entered,
    review_entered,
    payment_entered,
    confirmation_entered,
    ROUND((cart_entered - shipping_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(cart_entered, 0), 2) AS cart_to_shipping_drop_off,
    ROUND((shipping_entered - billing_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(shipping_entered, 0), 2) AS shipping_to_billing_drop_off,
    ROUND((billing_entered - review_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(billing_entered, 0), 2) AS billing_to_review_drop_off,
    ROUND((review_entered - payment_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(review_entered, 0), 2) AS review_to_payment_drop_off,
    ROUND((payment_entered - confirmation_entered) * 100.0 / NULLIF(payment_entered, 0), 2) AS payment_to_confirmation_drop_off
  FROM step_counts
)
SELECT
  cart_to_shipping_drop_off,
  shipping_to_billing_drop_off,
  billing_to_review_drop_off,
  review_to_payment_drop_off,
  payment_to_confirmation_drop_off
FROM funnel_metrics

title: Payment Failure Rate
when_to_use: monitor payment gateway health and provider reliability
diagnose checkout friction and lost sales
evaluate impact of payment method changes (new gateways, card networks)
identify customer segments with high failure rates (e.g., new cards, international)
trigger real-time recovery (e.g., alternative payment methods)
business_definition: Percentage of payment attempts that failed (declined, expired, or technical error) out of total payment attempts in the period. A payment attempt is defined as a submission to the payment processor, regardless of outcome.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The reliability and success rate of payment processing — a direct indicator of technical and fraud-related friction in the checkout flow.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Reason for failure. This metric quantifies *that* a failure occurred, but not *why* (e.g., insufficient funds, card expired, fraud block).
metric_nature.common_misconception: Payment failure rate is not the same as refund rate. Refunds occur post-fulfillment; payment failures occur *before* order confirmation and prevent fulfillment.

template: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) AS failed_payments, COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id) AS total_attempts, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM payments p WHERE p.payment_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}

inflation_causes.cause: Including duplicate payment attempts in failure count
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If retries are logged as new payment_id entries (e.g., same order retried 3 times), a single failed card is counted as 3 failures — inflating failure rate and masking true customer-level error rates.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT order_id, COUNT(*) AS attempts, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN payment_id END) AS failures FROM payments WHERE payment_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY order_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 ORDER BY attempts DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Deduplicate payment attempts per order using `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY payment_date DESC) = 1` to count only final attempts.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting partial payments (e.g., gift card + credit card split)
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a split payment uses two methods (e.g., $50 gift card + $50 credit), and only the credit card fails, it is counted as *one* failure — correct. However, if *both* are logged separately (e.g., as two payments), and one succeeds and one fails, it counts as *one* failure out of *two* attempts — inflating failure rate because only one partial attempt was needed to succeed.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT payment_id) AS payments, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN payment_id END) AS failures FROM payments WHERE payment_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY order_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT payment_id) > 1 ORDER BY payments DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Count failures as percentage of *successful orders* where any payment attempt failed — e.g., `COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_status = 'failed' THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0)`.

inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If third-party payment methods (e.g., PayPal, Apple Pay) are included but their failure semantics differ (e.g., PayPal declines before redirect, credit card declines at confirmation), the failure rate appears inflated due to inconsistent definitions.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT payment_method, COUNT(DISTINCT payment_id) AS attempts, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN payment_id END) AS failures FROM payments WHERE payment_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY attempts DESC
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to core card payments (or normalize failure definitions across methods) before aggregation.

deflation_causes.cause: Excluding 'soft' declines (e.g., fraud check pending, CVV mismatch)
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If only 'hard' declines (insufficient funds) are counted, but 'soft' declines (CVV, address mismatch, fraud block) are excluded, failure rate appears artificially low — masking preventable friction.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT decline_code, COUNT(DISTINCT payment_id) AS attempts FROM payments WHERE payment_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY decline_code ORDER BY attempts DESC
deflation_causes.fix: Include all non-success statuses (declined, expired, error) unless business defines 'failure' narrowly (e.g., only hard declines).

diagnostic_questions: Is the failure rate anomaly concentrated in specific payment methods or gateways?
Has a new fraud rule or 3DS2 mandate been introduced recently?
Are failures correlated with order value (e.g., high-value orders fail more)?
Did a change in card BIN ranges or international processing occur?
Are failures tied to specific customer segments (e.g., new cards, international)?

causal_relationships.if: Payment failure rate up, checkout_step_drop_off at payment up
causal_relationships.then: payment gateway issues (e.g., rate limits, maintenance) causing failures — likely technical, not customer-side

causal_relationships.if: Payment failure rate up, new_card_rate up
causal_relationships.then: new card introductions (e.g., post-launch, promotions) with unfamiliar BINs are triggering fraud blocks

causal_relationships.if: Payment failure rate down, fraud_rate up
causal_relationships.then: less aggressive fraud rules now allow more transactions — likely a balance shift toward lower rejection and higher true fraud

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Payment failure rate increases >25% WoW with no change in order volume
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A new fraud detection rule (e.g., address verification, velocity check) or gateway maintenance window is causing more payment rejections *and* pushing customers to abandon rather than retry.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', f.event_time) AS day, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN f.event = 'payment_declined' THEN f.session_id END) AS declines, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN f.event = 'abandon_checkout' THEN f.session_id END) AS abandonments, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN f.event = 'order_completed' THEN f.session_id END) AS conversions FROM events f WHERE f.event IN ('payment_declined', 'abandon_checkout', 'order_completed') AND f.event_time >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY day

cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A new high-value payment method (e.g., Klarna, Afterpay) or financing option was introduced, reducing declines for large orders — increasing AOV *and* reducing failure rate by diversifying payment options.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders, AVG(o.order_total) AS aov, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM orders o LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting failures as percentage of *orders*, not *payment attempts*
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If a multi-method payment fails on the first attempt but succeeds on retry, and only the successful order is counted, failure rate appears as 0% — masking the friction.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_status = 'failed' THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM orders
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM payments p WHERE p.payment_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'

anti_patterns.why_wrong: If order_status is only set after *all* payment attempts are exhausted, and failed orders are not immediately captured, payment failure rate appears lower than reality — masking near-term losses.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_status = 'failed' THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM payments p WHERE p.payment_date >= '2024-01-01'

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM payments p WHERE p.payment_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', p.payment_date) AS month, p.payment_method, COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id) AS attempts, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) AS failures, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN p.payment_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_id), 0) AS failure_rate FROM payments p WHERE p.payment_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, p.payment_method ORDER BY 1, 5 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH payment_attempts AS (
  -- Grain: payment attempt details
  SELECT
    payment_id,
    order_id,
    payment_method,
    status,
    payment_date,
    decline_code,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY payment_date DESC) AS attempt_rank
  FROM payments
  WHERE payment_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
),
final_attempts AS (
  SELECT * FROM payment_attempts WHERE attempt_rank = 1
),
failure_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS total_orders,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN order_id END) AS failed_orders,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN status IN ('declined', 'expired', 'error') THEN payment_id END) AS failed_payments
  FROM final_attempts
)
SELECT
  total_orders,
  failed_orders,
  failed_payments,
  ROUND(failed_orders * 100.0 / NULLIF(total_orders, 0), 2) AS order_failure_rate,
  ROUND(failed_payments * 100.0 / NULLIF(total_orders, 0), 2) AS payment_failure_rate
FROM failure_metrics

title: Search-to-Purchase Rate
when_to_use: assess search functionality quality and relevance
benchmark search performance across categories or seasons
diagnose drop-offs between search queries and purchases
evaluate impact of search UX or algorithm changes
estimate search-driven revenue contribution
business_definition: Percentage of search sessions (sessions with a search event) that resulted in a completed purchase in the same session or within a short follow-up window (e.g., 30 minutes or next session). A search session is defined as one with at least one search event.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: How effectively search drives purchases — a direct measure of search relevance, keyword quality, and conversion path from search to purchase.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Incrementality. This metric does not distinguish between search-driven purchases and purchases that would have happened anyway.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Search-to-purchase rate is not the same as organic search traffic conversion rate. It measures *within-site search* effectiveness, not *external* search engine traffic.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting purchases across sessions as part of search session
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the metric includes purchases from *subsequent sessions* (e.g., user searches today, purchases tomorrow), search sessions are attributed to future purchases — inflating conversion rate when users browse across sessions.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', o.order_date) AS order_day, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN search_session_date < o.order_date THEN o.session_id END) AS cross_session_purchases FROM orders o JOIN search_sessions s ON o.session_id = s.session_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY order_day
inflation_causes.fix: Constrain purchase attribution to the *same session* or a narrow follow-up window (e.g., ≤30 minutes after search event).

inflation_causes.cause: Including empty or irrelevant search queries
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If 'search' events are logged for empty inputs or system tests, and users who search 'a', 'test', 'help' are included in the denominator, search quality appears artificially high — especially if those users browse and purchase anyway.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT search_query, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_id IS NOT NULL THEN session_id END) AS purchases FROM search_events LEFT JOIN orders ON search_events.session_id = orders.session_id WHERE search_query IS NOT NULL GROUP BY search_query HAVING COUNT(*) > 10 ORDER BY sessions DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Filter search events to exclude empty, single-character, or known-test queries before aggregation.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting purchases *before* search in same session
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a session adds items to cart *before* the search event, and the purchase is counted as 'search to purchase', search is credited for purchases it did not drive — inflating rate.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT session_id, MIN(event_time) AS first_event, MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'search' THEN event_time END) AS search_time FROM events WHERE session_id IN (SELECT DISTINCT session_id FROM search_events) GROUP BY session_id HAVING MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'search' THEN event_time END) IS NULL OR MIN(event_time) < MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'search' THEN event_time END) ORDER BY search_time DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Require search event to occur *before* purchase in the same session: `search_event_time < purchase_event_time`.

deflation_causes.cause: Excluding mobile sessions with delayed search logs
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If mobile search events are logged late due to offline caching, the search appears after purchase — exclusion of the session inflates the denominator and deflates conversion rate.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event_type = 'search' AND event_time > purchase_time THEN session_id END) AS misordered_events FROM (SELECT session_id, MIN(CASE WHEN event_type = 'search' THEN event_time END) AS search_time, MIN(CASE WHEN event_type = 'order_completed' THEN event_time END) AS purchase_time FROM events WHERE event_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY session_id) sub
deflation_causes.fix: Attribute search events to sessions using persistent session_id and device_id, and allow a grace period (e.g., search within 10 minutes after session creation).

diagnostic_questions: Is the conversion rate anomaly concentrated in specific search categories or queries?
Has a new search algorithm, synonyms, or autocomplete feature been introduced?
Are high-conversion searches correlated with specific product types or prices?
Did mobile vs desktop search performance shift recently?
Are low-conversion searches due to poor relevance or intent mismatch?

causal_relationships.then: more search users + better quality — likely improved search UX or product catalog alignment
search traffic growth is lower quality — likely marketing campaigns driving untargeted traffic or keyword stuffing

causal_relationships.if: Search-to-purchase rate up, revenue per visitor down
causal_relationships.then: search-driven purchases are lower value — likely discount-driven searches (e.g., 'cheap') or high-add-to-cart, low-checkout behavior

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Search-to-purchase rate drops >15% MoM with stable traffic
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Search relevance has degraded — likely due to a catalog update, synonym removal, or algorithm change — causing users to search and leave without purchasing.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', s.search_date) AS week, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS searches, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS purchases, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN page_views = 1 AND page_exit = true THEN s.session_id END) AS bounces FROM search_sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id LEFT JOIN page_events p ON s.session_id = p.session_id WHERE s.search_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY week

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Search-to-purchase rate increases with no change in search volume
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A search algorithm optimization (e.g., improved vector search, semantic relevance) is driving more relevant results — increasing purchase likelihood without altering search volume.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', s.search_date) AS week, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS searches, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS purchases, AVG(p.relevance_score) AS avg_relevance FROM search_sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id JOIN product_search p ON s.search_query = p.search_query WHERE s.search_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY week

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting *all* search events as search sessions
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If multiple search events in one session are treated as multiple search sessions, the denominator inflates — deflating conversion rate.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.search_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.search_id), 0) AS rate FROM search_events s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM (SELECT DISTINCT session_id FROM search_events) s LEFT JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT session_id FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed') o ON s.session_id = o.session_id

anti_patterns.why_wrong: If search and purchase sessions are assigned IDs differently (e.g., new session on purchase), matching by ID fails — deflating conversion rate and masking true search influence.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN s.search_session_id = o.search_session_id THEN s.search_session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.search_session_id), 0) AS rate FROM search_events s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.search_session_id = o.search_session_id
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM search_sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id

sql_assets.basic_sql: WITH search_sessions AS (SELECT DISTINCT session_id FROM events WHERE event_type = 'search' AND event_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'), purchased_sessions AS (SELECT DISTINCT s.session_id FROM search_sessions s JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31') SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS search_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id) AS purchased_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT p.session_id) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS conversion_rate FROM search_sessions s LEFT JOIN purchased_sessions p ON s.session_id = p.session_id
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', s.session_date) AS month, s.search_query, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS searches, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS purchases, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS rate FROM search_sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id WHERE s.session_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, s.search_query ORDER BY 1, 5 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH search_sessions AS (
  -- Grain: sessions with search events, filtered to valid queries
  SELECT DISTINCT session_id, MIN(event_time) AS search_time
  FROM events
  WHERE event_type = 'search'
    AND event_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY session_id
),
purchased_sessions AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT session_id, MIN(event_time) AS purchase_time
  FROM events
  WHERE event_type = 'order_completed'
    AND event_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY session_id
),
matched AS (
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    s.search_time,
    p.purchase_time,
    DATEDIFF(minute, s.search_time, p.purchase_time) AS minutes_to_purchase
  FROM search_sessions s
  LEFT JOIN purchased_sessions p ON s.session_id = p.session_id
  WHERE p.purchase_time IS NOT NULL AND p.purchase_time >= s.search_time AND p.purchase_time <= DATEADD(minute, 30, s.search_time)
)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT m.session_id) AS purchased_sessions,
  COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS search_sessions,
  ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT m.session_id) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0), 2) AS conversion_rate,
  ROUND(AVG(minutes_to_purchase), 2) AS avg_minutes_to_purchase
FROM search_sessions s
LEFT JOIN matched m ON s.session_id = m.session_id

title: Cross-sell Attach Rate
when_to_use: assess cross-selling strategy effectiveness
benchmark attachment rates across product categories or merchants
optimize bundling, recommendations, or bundling pricing
identify high- or low-attachment products for inventory planning
measure revenue uplift from cross-selling initiatives
business_definition: Percentage of orders containing at least two distinct product categories (e.g., 'electronics' and 'home') out of total orders. A category is defined at the product level and aggregated to order level.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The frequency of multi-category purchases — a proxy for cross-selling success and customer willingness to buy disparate items in one transaction.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Value or profit contribution of attached items. An order may have two categories but only one high-margin item — attach rate doesn’t quantify economic impact.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Cross-sell attach rate is not the same as basket size. Attach rate requires *category* diversity; basket size measures *item* count regardless of category.

template: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) AS cross_sell_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS total_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS attach_rate FROM (SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT category) AS category_count FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY order_id) sub

inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If category hierarchy is flat (e.g., 'electronics', 'electronics/accessories', 'electronics/smartphones'), sub-categories are counted separately — an order with one main category and three sub-categories inflates category_count to 4.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT category, COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) AS products FROM products GROUP BY category ORDER BY products DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Use a normalized category taxonomy (e.g., parent category) or aggregate categories to a consistent level before counting.

inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If test orders with many categories (e.g., QA orders) are included, attachment rate appears higher — test orders may artificially inflate category diversity without commercial relevance.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT order_id, status, COUNT(DISTINCT category) AS categories FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'test' GROUP BY order_id, status HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT category) > 5 ORDER BY categories DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to completed or confirmed orders only, excluding test, fraud, and cancelled statuses.

inflation_causes.cause: Deduplicating category at product level instead of order level
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If category_count is computed before order aggregation (e.g., using `COUNT(DISTINCT category) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id)`), and then summed across line items, the same category is counted multiple times — inflating cross-sell orders.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT category) AS item_categories, COUNT(DISTINCT category) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id) AS order_categories FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id ORDER BY order_id LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: First aggregate to order level (`GROUP BY order_id`), *then* count distinct categories.

deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If digital or service items lack a category, they are excluded — orders with physical product + digital bundle appear as single-category, deflating attach rate.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category IS NULL THEN product_id END) AS uncategorized_products FROM products
deflation_causes.fix: Assign a default category (e.g., 'miscellaneous') or include uncategorized items in cross-sell count only if they are non-physical items.

diagnostic_questions: Is the attach rate anomaly concentrated in specific categories or product types?
Has a new recommendation engine, bundling offer, or upsell prompt been introduced?
Are attach rates higher for first-time buyers or repeat customers?
Did seasonal demand shifts affect category diversity (e.g., holiday bundles)?
Are low-attach categories due to product constraints or merchandising?

causal_relationships.if: Cross-sell attach rate up, AOV up
causal_relationships.then: cross-selling is driving higher basket size — likely successful recommendation algorithm or bundling strategy

causal_relationships.if: Cross-sell attach rate down, AOV up
causal_relationships.then: fewer categories but higher value per category — likely premiumization (e.g., high-price单品) or category focus

causal_relationships.if: Cross-sell attach rate up, return_rate up
causal_relationships.then: more diverse orders are harder to match expectations — likely over-selling or mismatched recommendations causing returns

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Cross-sell attach rate drops >10% MoM with stable order volume
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A recommendation engine refresh or algorithm change reduced suggestion relevance — users engaged less with cross-sell prompts and purchased fewer categories.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', r.event_time) AS week, COUNT(DISTINCT r.session_id) AS recommendations, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.event = 'click' THEN r.session_id END) AS clicks, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN r.session_id END) AS purchases FROM recommendation_events r LEFT JOIN orders o ON r.session_id = o.session_id WHERE r.event_time >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY week

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Cross-sell attach rate spikes during holiday season
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Seasonal bundling campaigns (e.g., gift packs, holiday bundles) are driving multi-category purchases with higher-margin items — increasing both attach rate and margin per order.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN p.is_holiday_bundled = true THEN o.order_id END) AS bundled_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.category_count >= 2 THEN o.order_id END) AS cross_sell_orders, AVG(nr.net_revenue) AS avg_nrv FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON o.order_id = nr.order_id LEFT JOIN (SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT category) AS category_count FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY order_id) c ON o.order_id = c.order_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

anti_patterns.pattern: Using item count instead of category count
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If attach rate is defined as orders with ≥2 items (not categories), orders with 10 identical items from the same category are misclassified as cross-sells — inflating rate and obscuring true category diversity.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN item_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS rate FROM (SELECT order_id, COUNT(*) AS item_count FROM order_items GROUP BY order_id) sub
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS rate FROM (SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT category) AS category_count FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY order_id) sub

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting category at line-item level without deduplication
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If category count is performed on order_items without aggregation, each line item counts as a separate 'order' with its own category — breaking the metric entirely.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS rate FROM (SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT category) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id) AS category_count FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id) sub
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS rate FROM (SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT category) AS category_count FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY order_id) sub

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0) AS attach_rate FROM (SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT category) AS category_count FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY order_id) sub
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, p.parent_category, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.category_count >= 2 THEN o.order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id), 0) AS attach_rate, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders FROM orders o JOIN (SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT p.category) AS category_count FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY order_id) c ON o.order_id = c.order_id JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, p.parent_category ORDER BY 1, 3 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH order_categories AS (
  -- Grain: one row per order with category diversity
  SELECT
    order_id,
    COUNT(DISTINCT p.category) AS category_count
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
    AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY order_id
),
order_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN category_count >= 2 THEN order_id END) AS cross_sell_orders,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS total_orders
  FROM order_categories
)
SELECT
  cross_sell_orders,
  total_orders,
  ROUND(cross_sell_orders * 100.0 / NULLIF(total_orders, 0), 2) AS attach_rate,
  ROUND(total_orders / NULLIF(cross_sell_orders, 0), 2) AS orders_per_cross_sell
FROM order_metrics
